og.title: HVAC Systems & Indoor Air Quality: For Technicians | CARSI
og.description: A free CARSI course showing technicians how HVAC systems shape indoor air quality — airflow, MERV and HEPA filtration, UV-C and carbon, ventilation, moisture and mould, ductwork and odour control.

faq.q: How do HVAC systems affect indoor air quality?
faq.a: HVAC systems condition and circulate air throughout a building, so they can either remove or spread contaminants. Poorly maintained units distribute dust, mould spores, VOCs and odours, while well-maintained systems filter and dilute pollutants and control humidity.

faq.q: What filter should a technician choose?
faq.a: The course explains the MERV scale (1–16) and HEPA filters, which capture very fine particles. Higher-rated filters clean more but restrict airflow, so the system must be compatible. UV-C lamps and activated carbon filters can supplement mechanical filtration.

faq.q: How does HVAC relate to moisture and mould?
faq.a: Cooling coils condense and drain moisture from the air, but dirty coils, clogged drain lines, overflowing condensate pans or oversized systems can leave excess humidity. Relative humidity above 60 per cent encourages mould, dust mites and material breakdown.
It is a free course for HVAC technicians and service professionals who want to assess, maintain and improve indoor air quality through HVAC performance. There are no prerequisites.
